title: Data-Driven Estimation of the Thinness Ratio from Time Series: Protocols and Uncertainty Quantification
author: Doucette, Doug
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This article presents a comprehensive methodological protocol for estimating the thinness ratio—a diagnostic that distinguishes between "thin chaos" (local instability with confined transport) and "thick chaos" (instability that leads to operational failure)—directly from experimental or observational time series, without requiring knowledge of the underlying governing equations.  
  The thinness ratio 𝒯(T) = σ(T)/δ compares chaotic displacement σ(T) accumulated over time T to the failure scale δ (the distance to a failure boundary). While readily computed from known equations, estimating this ratio from real-world data faces three core challenges: reconstructing the underlying state space from partial observations, identifying a meaningful transport coordinate, and handling finite-sample bias and uncertainty.  
  The protocol proceeds in five steps. First, delay-coordinate embedding reconstructs the state space, with embedding dimension determined by false-nearest-neighbors and delay by mutual information. Second, a transport-relevant coordinate is constructed within this space—typically via principal component analysis or other data-driven techniques—since the physically meaningful variable is rarely measured directly. Third, finite-time chaotic displacement is estimated as the root-mean-square displacement of this coordinate over windows of length T, and the failure scale δ is defined from observed data (e.g., as a high quantile of the coordinate's range). Fourth, the thinness ratio is computed as the ratio of these estimates. Fifth, uncertainty is quantified using block bootstrap resampling and surrogate-data testing, yielding confidence intervals that support statistical classification.  
  The protocol is validated on synthetic Lorenz system data, recovering the known ground-truth thinness ratio (≈0.27) with bootstrap confidence intervals containing the true value. It is then applied successfully to experimental voltage time series from a driven Duffing oscillator, correctly identifying persistently thin regimes and detecting the transition to thick chaos with lead time. Classification rules are provided: thick if the lower 95% confidence bound exceeds unity; thin if the upper bound lies below 0.3; transitional otherwise.  
  Key practical guidelines include systematic embedding parameter selection, comparing multiple candidate transport coordinates, explicitly justifying the failure-scale definition, mandatory uncertainty quantification, and verifying estimate stabilization with record length. Limitations include embedding distortion, arbitrariness in defining δ from data when physical thresholds are unknown, assumptions of mixing dynamics, and computational scaling.  
  The protocol converts the theoretical thin/thick distinction into a reproducible, uncertainty-quantified tool applicable to laboratory experiments, field observations, and engineering diagnostics, extending the framework beyond purely numerical or analytical settings into data-driven science.
